chapters.title: Let yourself off the hook and be free to enjoy life
chapters.text:
In the globalized world, you are free to be anyone you want. Every activity can turn into a serious income source. Your friends or acquaintances may have a gift for something specific, like accounting, working out, acting, etc. At the same time, you might have a job that brings food to the table but judge yourself for lacking spark and purpose. Surprisingly or not, people like you are the majority in our society.

You spend much of your time trying to please everyone else. It exhausts you mentally and physically, and that burden keeps you from enjoying life on your terms. On top of that, you drag your friends and family into that depressing abyss, too. Placing high expectations on yourself naturally overflows to those we love. After all, the threads of relation unite us with others.

That is why continuing such a life does not benefit you or the people around you. Therefore, learning to release the tension inflicted by society and yourself is critical. You’ll be free from fearing criticism and always trying to be perfect. Instead, you’ll learn to simply be.

Perfection discredits uniqueness.

When you give yourself a break and realize the worthlessness of perfection you’re free to enjoy your time and the people you care about. That is a big blessing for every mindful person.

Jen Hatmaker faced the same problems as a woman, mother, and professional. She shared her experience to help others relate and find the answers for themselves.

One of the best parts of being human is other humans. ~ Jen Hatmaker

chapters.title: You can’t have it all, but you can achieve balance
chapters.text:
How often do you get frustrated seeing everyone around you managing everything like a pro while you struggle to tick one thing off your to-do list? It seems like everyone knows the big secret but you. Ironically, we all pretend to be productive while gasping for air in the sea of tasks.

It's impossible to have and do it all, no matter what anyone tells you. What you can have, however, is balance.

People often ask Jen Hatmaker about juggling work, marriage, family, and her home. Her answer is balance. You often see successful movie characters and social media personalities effortlessly achieving all their goals in all areas of life, which creates an impossible standard. As a result, you feel like a failure chasing an unrealistic ideal.

Jen Hatmaker freely admits to receiving help from her team and family. She has people working for her, handling her bookings and paperwork. She also has a housekeeper and a part-time nanny. It's impossible to always be awesome, but it is possible to know your limitations, find a balance, and be wise enough to admit that it's enough.

The secret of productivity is avoiding standard templates and focusing on your unique approach.

Some parts of your life can drain you, so getting rid of them is better. Ask yourself what aspects you dread or find exhausting. If you're not sure, ask God through prayer or meditation. Once you know, set boundaries and stick to them. You'll be surprised that people respect your limits when you're firm enough.

For example:

• Does cooking for your family satisfy you? If not, share this duty with your spouse or children. Discuss other chores and redistribute them if necessary. Make time in the kitchen a possibility for family bonding.
• Does the afternoon coffee and gossip with your friends or colleagues make you uncomfortable? If you feel like you’re wasting time, rethink your everyday activities. It applies to all things that you regret later.
• Do you enjoy parent-teacher meetings? Search for alternatives if you prefer staying home with kids. You can also delegate this task to your spouse if they tolerate school meetings better.

Did you know? Attempting to be perfect and not understanding the importance of balance and boundaries is one of the leading causes of chronic stress and anxiety.

chapters.title: Quit searching for your calling and focus on a worthy life
chapters.text:
Jen Hatmaker believes that if some principles don’t relate to a poor single Christian mom in Haiti, they must be false. She lives by this belief, and you could apply the same vision.

For instance, if a sermon promises wealth to the faithful, she knows it isn't true because that would mean God only likes the wealthy. Similarly, suppose a scripture emphasizes women with children. But Jen knows that is false because some women cannot have children, not by choice but by circumstance.

Being critical of your beliefs is the best way to explain them to yourself and preserve tolerance.

Many people, especially parents, often put off their dreams for better times. Thoughts like “When my children grow up, then I will…,” ”When I have more free time…,” or “When my skills get better, then….” force them to shelve their ambitions. However, the proverbial poor mom in Haiti doesn't spend time trying to find her life's calling. Her only focus is working, looking after her children, and giving them a virtuous life. Therefore, she lives a worthy life, focusing on the present. She is doing her best now, not in a hypothetical future.

The idea is not to discourage you from searching for your calling but rather the opposite. However, the focus should be on the worth of whatever you do. This approach will bring you a sense of fulfillment you were putting off along with your mission.

On the other hand, don’t neglect your gifts, whether it’s singing, writing, acting, or anything else, don't neglect it. Look for inspiration and see how far it can take you. There is a genuine and clear difference between being humble about your skills and being insecure or overly critical of yourself.

Take the time to think about what you're good at and work on it. If you're unsure what that is, consult your friends and family. It's possible to earn a living from the thing you love doing. If you need to work on your talent or gift, go for it. Take an evening class, attend conferences, find a mentor, and say “yes” to things you usually refuse to do. Opening yourself to new experiences can reveal unexpected hobbies or inspirations. After all, you'll only know if you try.

Maybe your best thing won’t draw a paycheck, but it is how you shine and glow, and come to life and bless the world. ~ Jen Hatmaker

chapters.title: The lost sheep of the “distressed and struggling” flock
chapters.text:
It's easy to assume that life is much easier for everyone else. But the truth is that everyone is struggling. Some people just hide it better than others. It is incredibly human to make decisions and worry about their rightness. A lack of confidence often makes us panic about the choices we make.

We assume everyone else lives a life free of worry and confusion, but it's not the case. Everyone has doubts; everyone is confused and uncertain. However, being honest about your feelings is essential because people gravitate toward vulnerability. They feel safe opening up to someone genuine, undisguised, and thus reliable.

As a result, we can be there for one another, creating a support system and knocking down the presumption that life is simply easier for everyone else. It takes courage to admit your fears, especially to others. It takes strength to talk about your concerns openly. Life is hard, but we can conquer it together.

One of the most significant troubles that occupy most adults’ minds is parenting. Hatmaker divides families into two categories: sweet and spicy. For example, her family is spicy. They're loud, disorganized, sarcastic, and quite annoying with their sense of humor. So, when her family runs into a sweet family, she starts to wonder about things she does wrong as a parent. The sour feeling grows when the woman sees celebrity parents who seem to have their life under control.

The truth is, nothing is wrong, whether a family is sweet or spicy. People are doing the very best they can in the world of parenting. The latter is full of ups and downs, doubts, and worries. So, aiming at a “mostly good” benchmark is realistic; it's cruel to exert oneself when it's not necessary.

Children don't need perfect parents; they need them to be satisfied, energetic, and fulfilled. You might occasionally “slip,” expressing your genuine, unfiltered emotions in front of your children because you’re human. Apologizing and talking the issue through can teach kids about the real world more than a plastic smile on your face. It is what unites a family, whether sweet or spicy.

Becoming a parent makes you a “newborn” as well. Mistakes are unavoidable as you learn to care about yourself and your child.

chapters.title: Less drama, more compromise
chapters.text:
Twenty years of marriage was a considerable achievement in Jen Hatmaker’s life. Like anyone in long-lasting matrimony, she worked hard to fine-tune her relationship. Every romantic connection starts with a bit of spicy drama. However, it doesn’t work in the long run. Compromise is the only way to go further.

At the beginning of her marriage, Hatmaker aspired to be a perfect darling wife. However, both she and her husband had strong, opinionated personalities. At first, it might seem like drama was to settle in the young family. However, as time passed, Hatmaker realized the beauty of compromising. She also learned some lessons along the way.

Firstly, accept that neither you nor your spouse can be good at the same things. However, your talents can complement each other. Honesty is the cornerstone of every relationship, so being candid is critical. On the other hand, an occasional white lie can save your spouse’s nerves. Still, only honesty allows people to know each other’s vulnerabilities, so your relationship is good if you understand where lies are suitable and when they’re taboo.

Secondly, meet people outside of your joint friend group. Marriage does not mean life imprisonment with the same inmates. Sticking to your spouse 24/7 is unhealthy, as it blocks both of you from improving in other spheres.

Some people feed off our energy. You give away your resources when you’re with them instead of enriching yourself.

Speaking of other people, some of them are all about drama. They love it, they attract it, and they revel in it. These are not the types of people you need in your life.

Happiness is the subject of a severe societal rant. Toxic people are crafty at forcing guilt upon you for your successes and achievements, especially when they lack some themselves. They may create unnecessary drama to attract attention to their persona.

Society doesn’t encourage giving up on anything or anyone without a fight. But taking responsibility for people who exaggerate their problems, yet do nothing to improve, will harm you, not them. Worrying about their future or seeking revenge is useless, as you’ll continue wasting your time on the wrong people. Focus on your family and true friends instead.

chapters.title: Embrace your power as a woman and hold your head up high
chapters.text:
Growing up around people who love you is beautiful. But a proper blessing is watching them love others, witnessing their fulfillment, and learning from them. The special bonus is when you watch women bonding. Jen Hatmaker was lucky to grow up around her grandma’s and mother’s girlfriends. The female community is one of the most unique environments to grow up in as a little girl.

In her childhood and youth, the interpretation of a woman’s worth confused Hatmaker. Her Church taught her about the woman’s place in the Christian household, but her mother, grandmother, and their female friends did not fit the mold and thinking of these strong and inspiring women as wrong or unfitting was upsetting.

However, God is more generous than the Church portrays Him. Girls and women are half of His congregation and His creations. Ignoring or belittling women's impact, especially after they gained rights through relentless perseverance, is counterproductive for society. In churches worldwide, women persist despite restrictions and open threats, as in the reported cases of female oppression in China and the Middle East. Still, women fight sexism and promote revolutionary legislation, even when they are in danger.

According to Paul the Apostle and the New Testament, women could guide the congregation, prophesy, and teach along with men. No one could humiliate them, and they had no reason for shame. In other words, God gave women a role and a platform 一 much broader than a kitchen.

Women should hold their heads high and be proud of their strength and capabilities. W hen women earn money, they spend it on their families and communities. In other words, women have united societies since the dawn of time. It is something to be genuinely proud of.

God made women similar to Himself by granting them the power of creation.

These days, women are more blessed than ever. They don't have to choose between being strong, having a career, or being gentle and feminine. Women can have it all. They justly benefit from the fight and victories of feminism throughout history.

Therefore, never wait for permission to go for what you truly want. Never be ashamed of who you are. Understand your worth and never aim for less.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Life is full of misconceptions and confusion ; perhaps the worst is the obligation to be perfect. It's not possible to be perfect because perfection simply doesn't exist. It's a far better idea just to be yourself. There is no other you, after all!

Finding happiness in life means accepting help when you need it. It’s possible to support each other and stay vulnerable by opening up to those around you. Nobody should feel pressure to act a certain way or embody a specific idea of perfection.

A life of being someone you're not is energy wasted. Embrace your strengths and weaknesses, and focus on your aims and goals instead. You don't need to worry about anyone else's opinions of you, only those you hold of yourself. When you focus on your thoughts, you will automatically notice the stress of expectation melting away. There is no better feeling than that!

Happiness means dismissing our worries, joining forces with those around us, and accepting our vulnerability as a strength. Wouldn't life be boring if everything was shiny and perfect? You can find the greatest joys in the ups and downs as you journey through life. By embracing all of this, you’ll be happier, healthier, and open to new opportunities that come your way.

Try this


• Develop your interests step by step. Spend more time doing something you genuinely love.
• Start celebrating your successes by writing down three things you're grateful for or are proud of daily.
• Create a self-care plan. It can include cosmetic procedures, taking yourself on a date, and the like.

Read it till the end?
